nodes.text:
ATTACHMENT 6-F (4)
NEPA
Conformance with Comprehensive Plans and Zoning 1 The project involves replacing existing sewer lines. No new sewer lines will be constructed.
Compatibility and Urban Impact 1 This project will not encourage growth outside of the existing city limits.
Slope 1 Project will not significantly affect topography.
Erosion 1 Project will have temporary effect on erosion during construction activities. Contractor will be required to use Best Management Practices to prevent erosion during construction and will restore areas disturbed by construction activities to pre-construction condition.
Soil Suitability 1 The project will not construct any new structures. The project will rehabilitate existing structures.
Hazards and Nuisances including Site Safety 1 Project will not contribute to or increase hazards or nuisances.
Energy Consumption 2 Project will reduce energy consumption at the City wastewater treatment plant.
Noise - Contribution to Community Noise Levels 1 Project will not increase ambient noise levels.
Air Quality
Effects of Ambient Air Quality on Project and Contribution to Community Pollution Levels		 1 Project will neither affect ambient air quality nor contribute to community pollution levels. Finished project does produce air emissions.
Environmental Design
Visual Quality - Coherence, Diversity, Compatible Use and Scale		 1 All of the work will be subsurface and will not have a visual aesthetic component.
Socioeconomic
Code Source or Documentation
Demographic Character Changes 1 Project will not impact demographics.
Displacement 1 Project will not displace any residential or commercial properties.
Employment and Income Patterns 1 Project will not impact employment and income patterns.
Community Facilities
and Services Code Source or Documentation
Educational Facilities 1 Project does not impact education facilities.
Commercial Facilities 1 Project does not affect commercial facilities.
Health Care 1 Project does not affect health care.
Social Services 1 Project does not affect social services
Solid Waste 1 Project does not affect solid waste facilities.
Waste Water 2 Project improves the wastewater collection system by reducing infiltration and inflow to the system and reducing flows to the wastewater treatment facility. The project is identified in the 2006 Comprehensive Sewer Plan.
Storm Water 1 Project does not affect the City’s stormwater facilities.
Water Supply 1 The project does not affect water supply.

nodes.text:
Text from WAC 173-240-060 Inclusion Check Confirmation / Location
anticipated future permit.
The evaluation considers the potential for regulatory changes that could require more stringent treatment requirements during the operating life of the facility.
The evaluation confirms whether the level of treatment produces a discharge that complies with applicable water quality criteria in Chapter 173-201A WAC.
For municipal WWTPs, an analysis of conventional pollutants (BOD5, TSS, pH, dissolved oxygen, temperature) is included.
For municipal WWTPs, an analysis of pathogens (Enterococci, E. Coli, or Fecal Coliform bacteria) is included.
For municipal WWTPs, an analysis of toxic pollutants commonly present (ammonia, chlorine) is included.
An evaluation of the effects of industrial discharges on final effluent quality and potential for toxic materials to pass through is included. (If applicable)
If the receiving water is on the 303(d) list as impaired, the analysis includes the parameters identified in the impairment listing.
Design values align with waste load allocations established in an approved TMDL. (If applicable)
When Ecology has not established a waste load allocation for an impaired parameter, the analysis demonstrates no reasonable potential to cause or contribute to a water quality standard violation.
The report demonstrates whether the discharge will cause a measurable change in existing water quality at the boundary of any authorized chronic mixing zone (as defined by Ecology's measurable change criteria).
Consultation with regional Ecology staff to determine the level of analysis needed to comply with Antidegradation provisions (WAC 173-201A-300 to 330) has occurred and the analysis is included.
(e) A description of the receiving water, applicable water quality standards, and how water quality standards will be met at the boundary of any applicable dilution zone. (173-201A-10Q WAC) The name, location (river mile, latitude/longitude, waterway segment number, township/range, etc.), and water quality classification of the

nodes.text:
# Chapter 1: Introduction

## 1.1 Purpose and Need for the Plan  
*WAC 173-240-050 (a)*

The Town of Spangle seeks to develop a General Sewer Plan and Facility Plan to address current and future wastewater management needs in compliance with Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 173-240-050 and WAC 173-240-060. This plan will ensure that the Town’s wastewater collection and treatment systems continue to operate efficiently, meet regulatory requirements, and support planned growth over the next 20 years.

The development of this plan is driven by the need to:  
* Evaluate existing system performance, capacity, and infrastructure conditions.  
* Identify necessary improvements to address aging infrastructure, inflow and infiltration (I&I) concerns, and system deficiencies.  
* Assess future population growth and land use changes to ensure the system can accommodate projected demand.  
* Ensure compliance with Washington State Department of Ecology’s (Ecology) General Engineering Requirements and discharge standards.  
* Develop cost-effective and sustainable solutions for system expansion and upgrades.

## 1.2 System Ownership, Operation, and Maintenance  
*WAC 173-240-050 (b)*

# Summary of Findings and Conclusions

*Based upon our review of the project, it will not have a significant impact on the surrounding environment.*

## ALTERNATIVES TO THE PROPOSED ACTION  
### Alternatives and Project Modifications Considered  
[24 CFR 58.40(e), Ref. 40 CFR 1508.9]  
(Identify other reasonable courses of action that were considered and not selected, such as other sites, design modifications, or other uses of the subject site. Describe the benefits and adverse impacts to the human environment of each alternative and the reasons for rejecting it.)

The alternative that the City considered for this project was *in-situ pipe lining.*

This alternative was not viable because it is necessary to relocate many of the sewer pipes out of private properties and/or saturated areas.

### No Action Alternative [24 CFR 58.40(e)]  
(Discuss the benefits and adverse impacts to the human environment of not implementing the preferred alternative).

*No Action would lead the City to be in violation of their NPDES permit and potentially open themselves up to lawsuits. The flows to the treatment plant would keep increasing and the level of treatment of the sewer flows would decrease. This would increase the level of pollutants in the treatment plant effluent and be an adverse impact to the human environment.*

### Mitigation Measures Recommended [24 CFR 58.40(d), 40 CFR 1508.20]  
(Recommend feasible ways in which the proposal or external factors relating to the proposal should be modified in order to eliminate or minimize adverse environmental impacts.)

*The proposed project is expected to have minimal impact on the environment. The City will require the Construction Contractor to use best management practices to prevent environmental impacts. The finished project will not have a significant impact.*

## Additional Studies Performed  
(Attach studies or summaries)

* City of Tekoa Comprehensive Sewer Plan, November 2006.  
* City of Tekoa Infiltration and Inflow Analysis, August 2017

## List of Sources, Agencies and Persons Consulted  
[40 CFR 1508.9(b)]

* Washington Department of Archeology and Historic Preservation  
* Spokane Tribe of Indians  
* FEMA Flood Map Service Center  
* US Fish & Wildlife National Wetlands Inventory Map  
* Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife

# ATTACHMENT 6-F (2) ## NEPA ### Statement of Purpose and Need for the Proposal: [40 CFR 1508.9(b)]: The City of Tekoa completed a Comprehensive Sewer System Plan in November 2006, in which numerous improvements to the sewer system were identified. The preparation of the plan was a requirement of the City’s NPDES permit. The plan identified several improvements to the system that were required to meet NPDES permit requirements to reduce infiltration and inflow. Many of the improvements in the plan were sewer line replacements along Hangman Creek. There are also several improvements across the city in areas with seasonally high groundwater. This project is needed to reduce infiltration and inflow to the sewer system and reduce the influent flows to the treatment facility to meet NPDES permit requirements. ### Description of the Proposal: Include all contemplated actions, which logically are either geographically, or functionally a composite part of the project, regardless of the source of funding. [24 CFR 58.32, 40 CFR 1508.25] *Some of the options considered for the sewer line rehabilitation include in-situ pipe lining, rerouting the sewer lines to existing right-of-way, and replacing the sewer lines along the same routes.* ### Existing Conditions and Trends: Describe the existing conditions of the project area and its surroundings, and trends likely to continue in the absence of the project. [24 CFR 58.40(a)] *The project occurs in three separate areas of the City and occurs mostly along existing roadways.* *There is a 5 block stretch of sewer slated for replacement along Washington, Poplar, and Crosby Streets, which includes a residential area, a park, Highway-27 and commercial businesses.* *An approximate 2 block length of sewer is slated to be replaced from Warren Street, through vegetation and along an access road behind the baseball field, along Howard Street, and to Seamon Road.* *Approximately 1700 LF of sewer will be replaced along East Main Street And Lindsey Street. Both stretches occur within residential areas.* *The area surrounding Tekoa is mostly agricultural farmland.* *The project is a replacement of existing sewer from existing areas, typically outside of Public ROW to Public ROW. If an existing sewer is located outside of the right-of-way, it will be replaced within the right-of-way.* ---- ### Statutory Checklist [24CFR §58.5] Record the determinations made regarding each listed statute, executive order or regulation. Provide appropriate source documentation. [Note reviews or consultations completed as well as any applicable permits or approvals obtained or required. Note dates of contact or page references]. Provide compliance or consistency documentation. Attach additional material as appropriate. Note conditions, attenuation or mitigation measures required.
Factors Determinations and Compliance Documentation
Historic Preservation
[36 CFR 800]		 Project will not threaten or demolish historic or culturally important properties.

DAHP Letter: March 30th, 2010
Spokane Tribe Letter: March 25th, 2010

# FINDING OF CATEGORICAL EXEMPTION (SEPA) It is the Finding of The City of Tekoa, WA that the following activities are Categorically Exempt under the Washington State Environmental Policy Act (SEPA) (RCW 43.21C.110 and WAC 197.11.800). (NOTE: If project is Categorically Exempt under SEPA, the SEPA Checklist is not required.) ## Location of Activities: * _The project area is located within The City of Tekoa, including portions of Section 13 and Section 24, Township 20 North, Range 45 East and Section 19, Township 20 North, Range 46 East. It includes areas in the project description below._ ## Description of Activities: * _The project will replace approximately 3,900 linear feet of sanitary sewer pipe and associated manholes that are experiencing extremely high amounts of infiltration and inflow. Sewer lines to be replaced include those along East Main Street and Lindsey Street, North Crosby Street and Poplar Street, and along the ballpark from Warren Street to Seamon Road._ ## Reasons for Categorical Exemption under SEPA: **WAC 197-11-800 (3):** > “The following activities shall be categorically exempt: The repair, remodeling, maintenance, or minor alteration of existing private or public structures, facilities or equipment, including utilities, recreation, and transportation facilities involving no material expansions or changes in use beyond that previously existing.”
Date: ___________________ ________________________________________
Signature of Environmental Review Officer

Signature of Chief Administrative Official

# FINDING OF CATEGORICAL EXEMPTION (SEPA) It is the Finding of The City of Tekoa, WA that the following activities are Categorically Exempt under the Washington State Environmental Policy Act (SEPA) (RCW 43.21C.110 and WAC 197.11.800). (NOTE: If project is Categorically Exempt under SEPA, the SEPA Checklist is not required.) ## Location of Activities: * _The project area is located within The City of Tekoa, including portions of Section 13 and Section 24, Township 20 North, Range 45 East and Section 19, Township 20 North, Range 46 East. It includes areas in the project description below._ ## Description of Activities: * _The project will replace approximately 3,900 linear feet of sanitary sewer pipe and associated manholes that are experiencing extremely high amounts of infiltration and inflow. Sewer lines to be replaced include those along East Main Street and Lindsey Street, North Crosby Street and Poplar Street, and along the ballpark from Warren Street to Seamon Road._ ## Reasons for Categorical Exemption under SEPA: **WAC 197-11-800 (3):** > “The following activities shall be categorically exempt: The repair, remodeling, maintenance, or minor alteration of existing private or public structures, facilities or equipment, including utilities, recreation, and transportation facilities involving no material expansions or changes in use beyond that previously existing.”

# ATTACHMENT 6-F (2) ## NEPA ### Statement of Purpose and Need for the Proposal: [40 CFR 1508.9(b)]: *The City of Tekoa completed a Comprehensive Sewer System Plan in November 2006, in which numerous improvements to the sewer system were identified.* The preparation of the plan was a requirement of the City’s NPDES permit. The plan identified several improvements to the system that were required to meet NPDES permit requirements to reduce infiltration and inflow. Many of the improvements in the plan were sewer line replacements along Hangman Creek. There are also several improvements across the city in areas with seasonally high groundwater. This project is needed to reduce infiltration and inflow to the sewer system and reduce the influent flows to the treatment facility to meet NPDES permit requirements. ### Description of the Proposal: Include all contemplated actions, which logically are either geographically, or functionally a composite part of the project, regardless of the source of funding. [24 CFR 58.32, 40 CFR 1508.25] *Some of the options considered for the sewer line rehabilitation include in-situ pipe lining, rerouting the sewer lines to existing right-of-way, and replacing the sewer lines along the same routes.* ### Existing Conditions and Trends: Describe the existing conditions of the project area and its surroundings, and trends likely to continue in the absence of the project. [24 CFR 58.40(a)] *The project occurs in three separate areas of the City and occurs mostly along existing roadways.* *There is a 5 block stretch of sewer slated for replacement along Washington, Poplar, and Crosby Streets, which includes a residential area, a park, Highway-27 and commercial businesses.* *An approximate 2 block length of sewer is slated to be replaced from Warren Street, through vegetation and along an access road behind the baseball field, along Howard Street, and to Seamon Road.* *Approximately 1700 LF of sewer will be replaced along East Main Street And Lindsey Street. Both stretches occur within residential areas.* *The area surrounding Tekoa is mostly agricultural farmland.* The project is a replacement of existing sewer from existing areas, typically outside of Public ROW to Public ROW. If an existing sewer is located outside of the right-of-way, it will be replaced within the right-of-way. ---- ### **Statutory Checklist** [24CFR §58.5] Record the determinations made regarding each listed statute, executive order or regulation. Provide appropriate source documentation. [Note reviews or consultations completed as well as any applicable permits or approvals obtained or required. Note dates of contact or page references]. Provide compliance or consistency documentation. Attach additional material as appropriate. Note conditions, attenuation or mitigation measures required.
Factors Determinations and Compliance Documentation
Historic Preservation
[36 CFR 800]		 Project will not threaten or demolish historic or culturally important properties.

DAHP Letter: March 30th, 2010
Spokane Tribe Letter: March 25th, 2010

# I. REGULATORY REQUIREMENTS FOR FACILITY PLAN/ENGINEERING REPORTS This Wastewater Treatment Plant Facility Plan (Facility Plan) has been prepared for the City of Tekoa, Washington, in accordance with the provisions of Washington Administrative Code (WAC) Chapter 173-240, "Submission of Plans and Reports for Construction of Wastewater Facilities." The WAC requirements for an engineering report (as detailed in WAC 173-240-060) are listed in the table below, cross-referenced to their location within this document. Technical guidance was also drawn from the Washington State Department of Ecology's (Ecology) "Criteria for Sewage Works Design" (Orange Book).
Text from WAC 173-240-060 Inclusion Check Confirmation / Location
060(1) Planning Requirements
The engineering report for a domestic wastewater facility shall include each appropriate (as determined by Ecology) item required in WAC 173-240-050 for general sewer plans unless an up-to-date general sewer plan is on file with Ecology. Normally, an engineering report is not required for sewer line extensions or pump stations. See WAC 173-240-020(13) and 173-240-030(5). The facility plan described in 40 CFR 35 is an “engineering report." The report complies with an up-to-date general sewer plan (WAC 173-240-050) approved by and on file with Ecology, OR the report includes required portions of WAC 173-240-050 as determined through consultation with Ecology if an adequate plan is not on file. No
General sewer plan adequately addresses the current conditions and service area. (If applicable and plan is on file) Yes
060(2) Sufficiently Complete
The engineering report shall be sufficiently complete so that plans and specifications can be developed from it without substantial changes. The report contains sufficient design information to allow an engineer not involved in writing the report to produce construction drawings for the facility without reevaluation of the selected unit processes or more than minor unit sizing modifications. Yes
060(3) Minimum Information Required
(a) The name, address, and telephone number of the owner of the proposed facilities, and their authorized representative. The report includes the name, address, and telephone number of the owner and the owner's authorized representative.

The named authorized representative or position has the authority to sign contracts relating to this project.

A specific project contact person other than the legal representative is identified. 		Section 1.2
(b) A project description including a location map and a map of the present and proposed service area. The project description includes the “where, what, and why” of the report and documents the need for the proposed project.

Planning information established in the approved general sewer plan is referenced, and supplemented or updated as necessary to support

nodes.text:
# 1 INTRODUCTION

This Wastewater Treatment Plant Facility Plan (Facility Plan) provides a comprehensive assessment and strategic roadmap for the City of Tekoa, Washington's wastewater infrastructure over the next 20 years. It thoroughly evaluates the existing collection, treatment, and disposal systems, identifies current deficiencies and future needs, and recommends the most cost-effective and compliant path forward. The analysis includes a detailed comparison of potential improvement scenarios against a no-action alternative to guide the City's decisions on essential wastewater system investments.

In addition, this report fulfills the Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 173-240-050, which requires an up to date General Sewer Plan in order to consider changes to the existing system such as the ones proposed in this Engineering Report.

## 1.1 Regulatory Requirements

This Facility Plan is developed in accordance with the requirements outlined in Washington Administrative Code (WAC) Chapter 173-240, "Submission of Plans and Reports for Construction of Wastewater Facilities," particularly WAC 173-240-060 which details the required engineering report content. It also considers design standards and guidance from the Washington State Department of Ecology's (Ecology) "Criteria for Sewage Works Design" (Orange Book), relevant surface water quality standards (WAC 173-201A), groundwater quality standards (WAC 173-200), and federal secondary treatment regulations. The table presented in the preliminary section cross-references specific WAC 173-240-060 requirements to their location within this report.
